reason: The item cleanly targets admissible mechanistic/diagnostic inferences from an ordered gas-evolution sequence with an explicit temperature-gap cue, and most options hinge on respecting (or violating) that boundary. Decoys are engineering-plausible and fail for distinct single reasons (sequence inversion, unsupported temperature inference, and scope overclaim), producing meaningful expert discrimination. A minor weakness is that some conclusions depend on assuming temporal order maps directly to temperature order in-field (sensor placement/mixing could confound), slightly softening boundary tightness and discriminative strength.

This is a short-answer item with no answer options, so decoy craftsmanship and incorrect-option diagnostics cannot be evaluated, collapsing D2 and weakening discriminative value. While the prompt targets a coherent mechanism (optical path loss vs gas cessation) plus an architectural implication, it bundles three sub-requests that invite partial-credit ambiguity and makes it hard to distinguish boundary-aware expertise from general sensor-common-sense responses. The lack of structured response constraints (e.g., required mentions, acceptable alternatives, and how to treat other plausible optical-failure mechanisms) further reduces boundary integrity and scoring reliability.
